questions.question_text: A study finds that cities with more fire trucks also have more fires. Does this mean fire trucks cause fires?

options.text: Yes, more fire trucks lead to more fires
No, a lurking variable (city size) likely explains both
Yes, the correlation proves causation
There is no correlation between fire trucks and fires

questions.explanation: Larger cities have both more fire trucks and more fires simply because of their size. City population is a lurking variable that explains the correlation without a causal link.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the difference between correlation and causation?

options.text: Correlation means one variable causes the other; causation means they are related
Correlation indicates a relationship between two variables; causation means one variable directly causes a change in the other
Correlation and causation mean the same thing
Causation can exist without any correlation

questions.explanation: Correlation shows that two variables are related, but causation requires that one variable directly produces a change in the other. Correlation alone does not prove causation.

questions.question_text: Ice cream sales and drowning incidents are positively correlated. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: Eating ice cream causes drowning
Drowning causes people to buy ice cream
A confounding variable, such as warm weather, causes both to increase
There is no correlation between them

questions.explanation: Warm weather is a confounding variable that increases both ice cream sales and swimming activity, which leads to more drowning incidents. The ice cream does not cause the drownings.

questions.question_text: Which type of study is best for establishing causation?

options.text: An observational study
A survey
A controlled experiment
A scatter plot analysis

questions.explanation: A controlled experiment, where variables are manipulated while others are held constant, is the best method for establishing a cause-and-effect relationship.

questions.question_text: A student notices that students who eat breakfast tend to have higher grades. Can the student conclude that eating breakfast causes higher grades?

options.text: Yes, the data proves it
No, other factors like overall family support or sleep habits could explain both
Yes, because there is a positive correlation
No, because the correlation is negative

questions.explanation: This is an observational finding, not a controlled experiment. Confounding variables like family support, sleep, and socioeconomic status could explain both habits.

questions.question_text: A newspaper headline states: 'Shoe size correlated with reading ability in children.' What is the most reasonable explanation?

options.text: Having bigger feet helps children read better
Reading more causes feet to grow
Age is a confounding variable; older children have bigger feet and better reading skills
There is no real correlation

questions.explanation: Age is the confounding variable. As children grow older, both their shoe size and reading ability increase, creating a spurious correlation between the two.

questions.question_text: Which statement is true about correlation and causation?

options.text: Correlation always implies causation
Causation always implies correlation
Causation never involves correlation
Correlation and causation are unrelated concepts

questions.explanation: If one variable truly causes a change in another, there will be a correlation between them. However, the reverse is not true: a correlation does not necessarily mean one causes the other.

questions.question_text: A study finds a strong positive correlation between the number of books in a home and children's test scores. A reporter writes: 'Buying more books boosts test scores.' What error is the reporter making?

options.text: Assuming causation from correlation
Assuming negative correlation
Ignoring the data entirely
Using the wrong type of graph

questions.explanation: The reporter is assuming causation from correlation. Other factors such as parental education or income level could be confounding variables that explain both.

questions.question_text: In a randomized controlled trial, one group receives a new medication and the other receives a placebo. If the medication group shows significant improvement, what can we conclude?

options.text: There is only a correlation, not causation
The medication likely caused the improvement because confounding variables were controlled
The placebo caused the improvement
No conclusion can be drawn from the experiment

questions.explanation: A randomized controlled trial controls for confounding variables by randomly assigning subjects. If the treatment group shows improvement, it is strong evidence of a causal relationship.

questions.question_text: Countries with more televisions per capita also have higher life expectancies. Does this mean televisions increase life expectancy?

options.text: Yes, watching TV improves health
No, wealthier countries can afford both more TVs and better healthcare
Yes, because the correlation is positive
There is no relationship between them

questions.explanation: National wealth is a confounding variable. Wealthier countries tend to have both more consumer electronics and better healthcare, creating a correlation that does not imply causation.

questions.question_text: What does 'causation' mean?

options.text: Two variables are related but one does not affect the other
A change in one variable directly produces a change in another variable
Two variables move in opposite directions
Two variables have no relationship

questions.explanation: Causation means that a change in one variable directly causes or produces a change in another variable, establishing a cause-and-effect relationship.

questions.question_text: What is a confounding variable?

options.text: A variable that is measured on the y-axis
A hidden or unmeasured variable that influences both the independent and dependent variables, creating a misleading association
A variable that has no effect on the study
The variable deliberately changed by the researcher

questions.explanation: A confounding variable is an unmeasured or uncontrolled factor that affects both variables being studied, potentially creating a false or misleading association between them.

questions.question_text: What does the phrase 'correlation does not imply causation' mean?

options.text: If two variables are correlated, one must cause the other
Just because two variables are related does not mean one directly causes the other
Correlation and causation are the same concept
Causation can never be proven

questions.explanation: This principle states that observing a statistical relationship between two variables is not sufficient to conclude that one causes the other. Other explanations, such as confounding variables, may exist.

questions.question_text: What is a lurking variable?

options.text: A variable that is directly measured in the study
A variable not included in the study that may affect the relationship between the studied variables
The dependent variable in an experiment
A variable with zero correlation

questions.explanation: A lurking variable is one that is not included in the analysis but may influence the relationship between the variables being studied, potentially leading to incorrect conclusions.

questions.question_text: What does 'spurious correlation' mean?

options.text: A genuine causal relationship between two variables
A correlation that appears to exist between two variables but is actually caused by a third factor or by coincidence
A very strong positive correlation
A correlation coefficient of exactly 1

questions.explanation: A spurious correlation is a statistical relationship that appears meaningful but is actually due to coincidence or the influence of a confounding variable, not a true causal connection.

questions.question_text: What is an observational study?

options.text: A study in which the researcher manipulates variables to test a hypothesis
A study in which the researcher observes and records data without manipulating any variables
A study that only uses surveys
A study that always proves causation

questions.explanation: In an observational study, the researcher collects data by observing subjects without manipulating any variables. This type of study can show correlation but generally cannot prove causation.

questions.question_text: What does 'controlled experiment' mean?

options.text: An experiment where no variables are measured
An experiment where the researcher manipulates one variable while keeping others constant to test for a cause-and-effect relationship
An experiment that only uses observational data
An experiment with no control group

questions.explanation: A controlled experiment involves manipulating an independent variable while holding all other variables constant, allowing the researcher to establish whether a causal relationship exists.

questions.question_text: What does 'random assignment' mean in an experiment?

options.text: Participants choose which group they want to be in
Participants are placed into treatment or control groups by chance to reduce the effect of confounding variables
The researcher selects specific participants for each group
All participants receive the same treatment

questions.explanation: Random assignment places participants into groups by chance, ensuring that confounding variables are evenly distributed across groups so that differences in outcomes can be attributed to the treatment.

questions.question_text: What is a 'placebo' in an experimental study?

options.text: A treatment that has an inactive substance or no real effect, used as a control
The actual medication being tested
A statistical test used to measure correlation
A confounding variable

questions.explanation: A placebo is an inactive treatment (such as a sugar pill) given to the control group to compare against the actual treatment, helping researchers isolate the true effect of the treatment.

questions.question_text: What does 'third variable problem' mean?

options.text: The problem of having too many data points
The possibility that an unmeasured third variable is responsible for the observed correlation between two other variables
The inability to collect more than two variables
The error caused by using the wrong formula

questions.explanation: The third variable problem occurs when an unmeasured variable influences both of the studied variables, making it appear as though they are directly related when the relationship is actually driven by the third variable.

questions.question_text: A health magazine reports: 'People who drink more coffee live longer — coffee increases lifespan!' A statistician objects. Which is the best reason for the objection?

options.text: Coffee drinkers may have other healthy habits (exercise, diet) that explain the longer lifespan, so correlation does not prove causation
Coffee is harmful, so the data must be wrong
The study must have had too few participants
There is no correlation between coffee and lifespan

questions.explanation: Coffee drinkers may also exercise more, eat better, or have higher incomes that contribute to longer life. These confounding variables make it incorrect to claim coffee itself causes a longer lifespan.

questions.question_text: A school district finds that students who participate in band also have higher math scores. The superintendent proposes requiring all students to join band. What is the flaw in this reasoning?

options.text: There is no flaw; band clearly improves math scores
The superintendent is confusing correlation with causation; students in band may already be high achievers
Band students have lower math scores
The district did not use a scatter plot

questions.explanation: Students who choose band may already be motivated, disciplined, or academically strong. The correlation between band participation and math scores does not prove that band causes higher math performance.

questions.question_text: A study shows a strong positive correlation between the number of hours spent on social media and levels of anxiety among teenagers. A parent group demands that social media be banned to reduce anxiety. What critical thinking principle should they consider?

options.text: Correlation proves causation, so the ban is justified
Anxious teenagers may seek social media as a coping mechanism, so the direction of causation is unclear
There is no correlation, so nothing should be done
The only way to reduce anxiety is medication

questions.explanation: The direction of causation is unclear: social media might contribute to anxiety, but anxious teens might also turn to social media. Without a controlled experiment, the causal direction cannot be determined.

questions.question_text: Researchers find that towns with more churches have higher crime rates. A journalist writes: 'Religion leads to crime.' What lurking variable most likely explains this correlation?

options.text: The type of religion practiced
Population size: larger towns have both more churches and more crime
Churches cause people to commit crimes
There is no lurking variable

questions.explanation: Population size is the lurking variable. Larger towns naturally have more of everything, including churches and crimes. The journalist's claim of causation is unfounded.

questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company tests a new drug using a double-blind randomized controlled trial and finds that the drug group shows significantly better outcomes than the placebo group. Why is this stronger evidence of causation than an observational study?

options.text: Because the sample size is always larger in experiments
Because random assignment and blinding control for confounding variables, isolating the drug's effect
Because experiments use scatter plots while observational studies do not
Because the company sponsored the study

questions.explanation: Random assignment distributes confounding variables evenly between groups, and blinding prevents bias. This design isolates the effect of the drug, providing strong evidence of causation.

questions.question_text: Data shows that countries that consume more chocolate per capita win more Nobel Prizes. A blogger claims: 'Eating chocolate makes you smarter.' Identify the most likely confounding variable.

options.text: The type of chocolate consumed
National wealth and investment in education
The number of chocolate factories
There is no confounding variable; chocolate causes intelligence

questions.explanation: Wealthier nations tend to consume more luxury goods (including chocolate) and also invest more in education and research, producing more Nobel laureates. National wealth is the confounding variable.

questions.question_text: A fitness study observes that people who own dogs walk more and have lower blood pressure. Can the study conclude that owning a dog causes lower blood pressure?

options.text: Yes, the study proves causation
No, because this is an observational study and confounding variables (like overall lifestyle) may explain the results
Yes, because the correlation is positive
No, because there is no correlation between dog ownership and blood pressure

questions.explanation: This is an observational study. People who own dogs may already lead more active lifestyles or have lower stress levels for other reasons. Without a controlled experiment, causation cannot be established.

questions.question_text: A city notices that neighborhoods with more streetlights have lower crime rates. The mayor proposes installing streetlights everywhere to reduce crime. What should the city consider before making this decision?

options.text: The proposal is perfect because correlation equals causation
Wealthier neighborhoods may have both more streetlights and lower crime, so a controlled study would help determine if streetlights actually reduce crime
Streetlights definitely cause crime to decrease
Crime rates have nothing to do with neighborhood conditions

questions.explanation: Neighborhood wealth is a potential confounding variable: wealthier areas may invest in both streetlights and security. A controlled study would help determine whether streetlights themselves reduce crime.

questions.question_text: A university finds that students who sit in the front row earn higher grades. A professor requires all students to sit in the front row next semester. What is wrong with this approach?

options.text: Nothing; front-row seating improves grades
Motivated students may choose to sit in front, so front-row seating may not cause higher grades
The correlation is negative, not positive
Seating location has no measurable correlation with grades

questions.explanation: Students who voluntarily sit in the front may already be more engaged and motivated. The correlation between seating and grades may be driven by student motivation, not the seat location itself.

questions.question_text: A tech company observes that employees who take more vacation days are more productive. Management considers mandating additional vacation. Before doing so, they should consider which of the following?

options.text: Nothing; the correlation proves mandating vacation will work
High performers may feel more comfortable taking vacation because they are already productive, making the direction of causation unclear
Vacation days have no correlation with productivity
Only negative correlations matter for policy decisions

questions.explanation: The direction of causation is ambiguous. Productive employees may feel more secure taking vacation, rather than vacation causing productivity. A controlled study would be needed to establish the causal direction.
